data.title: Oil heads for weekly rise as US adds sanctions on Iran, OPEC cuts
data.description:
Key points:
  • US issues new sanctions on Chinese importers of Iranian oil
  • Iraq, Kazakhstan, others to cut output more in OPEC plan
  • WTO slashes 2025 trade growth forecast, warns of deeper slump

Oil prices extended gains on Thursday on the prospect of tighter supply after Washington imposed further sanctions to curb Iranian oil trade and as some OPEC producers pledged further output cuts to compensate for pumping above agreed quotas.

Brent crude futures rose 34 cents, or 0.5%, to $66.19 a barrel by 0029 GMT, and U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude was at $62.91 a barrel, up 44 cents, or 0.7%.

Both benchmarks settled 2% higher on Wednesday at their highest levels since April 3 and are on track for their first weekly rise in three. Thursday is the last settlement day of the week ahead of the Good Friday and Easter holidays.

President Donald Trump's administration issued new sanctions targeting Iran's oil exports on Wednesday, including against a China-based "teapot" oil refinery, ramping up pressure on Tehran amid talks on the country's escalating nuclear programme.

Adding to supply concerns, the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) said on Wednesday it has received updated plans for Iraq, Kazakhstan and other countries to make further output cuts to compensate for pumping above quotas.

"(These factors) certainly could have affected sentiment – would argue that Iranian production (is) not significant and that OPEC quotas more often breached than observed, but both factors fed into the more bullish tone," said Michael McCarthy, CEO of online investment platform Moomoo.

Big draws on U.S. gasoline and distillates stocks and a smaller than expected gain in weekly crude inventories also bolstered markets, he said.

"Much of the recent selling pressure in global crude markets related to fears of an imminent flood of U.S. oil, but the drop in refining suggests that bottlenecks to supply may be emerging," McCarthy said.

Still, OPEC, the International Energy Agency and several banks, including Goldman Sachs and JP Morgan, cut forecasts on oil prices and demand growth this week as U.S. tariffs and retaliation from other countries threw global trade into disarray.

The World Trade Organization said it expected trade in goods to fall by 0.2% this year, down from its expectation in October of a 3.0% expansion.

data.title: Australia's gold stocks hit record high as bullion prices soar
data.description:
** Australian gold stocks rise as much as 2.9% to record high level

** Sub-index rising for sixth consecutive session; last up 1.3%

** Gold prices extended their stellar gains as a weaker dollar and escalating U.S.-China trade tensions pushed investors towards the safe-haven asset

** Gold miners Evolution Mining and Northern Star Resources up 2.5% and 1.6%, respectively

** Sub-index up 49.2% this year, as of last close

data.title: Bank of Korea Keeps Rates on Hold as Tariff Uncertainty Rises
data.description:
By Kwanwoo Jun

South Korea's central bank held interest rates steady in April, taking a widely anticipated breather as it gauges how best to support an economy facing a severe hit from U.S. tariffs.

The Bank of Korea kept its benchmark seven-day repurchase rate unchanged at 2.75% on Thursday, pausing its easing cycle after making cuts in February this year, and in October and November of 2024.

Twenty out of 25 economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal ahead of the decision had forecast no rate change in April, expecting uncertainty over President Trump's erratic trade announcements to make BOK policymakers cautious. The central bank likely also wants more time to assess the impact of prior rate cuts before making any more moves.

Asia's fourth-largest economy relies on exports for nearly half of its gross domestic product growth, leaving it exposed to trade disruptions from the sweeping U.S. tariffs announced recently.

Central banks in New Zealand, Singapore, India and the Philippines have recently eased policy settings, citing uncertainty around how U.S. trade policy will affect the global economy.

Asia stands to be particularly hard-hit by a slowdown in trade, and many economists have lowered growth outlooks for export-reliant countries like South Korea.

South Korea's state-run think tank cut its growth forecast for the country this year in February, citing the risk of new Trump tariffs. Downgrades from the likes of JPMorgan and Citigroup have followed, and the BOK is widely expected to lower the outlook for the country again in May after having cut its 2025 GDP growth forecast in February to 1.5% from 1.9%.

Write to Kwanwoo Jun at kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com

data.title: Australia's Santos gains on Q1 production rise
data.description:

** Oil and gas producer says Q1 production up 2% q/q, driven by higher output from Western Australia

** Energy sector up 2.3%

** YTD, STO down more than 17% this year

data.title: US Tariffs Could Add to Risks for Banking Sectors in Asia-Pacific Countries, Fitch Ratings Says
data.description:
Fitch Ratings had anticipated broadly stable operating conditions for the banking sectors in the Asia-Pacific region this year, but an increase in tariffs levied by the US could add to the risks faced by these sectors, according to a Wednesday report by the ratings firm.

The "neutral" banking sector outlooks for countries in the Asia-Pacific region, which generally have lower direct export exposure to the US, will be more resilient in the face of a higher US tariff regime. This includes Australia and New Zealand.

There is still risk in such markets that bank asset quality in specific sectors could be negatively impacted by further increases in US tariffs, or that they could be hit by indirect trade effects, such as weakening economic growth in China.

The impact of the trade tensions on the banking systems will depend on final tariff outcomes, their impact on local economic growth, the banks' exposure to vulnerable sectors, and the potential for changes in fiscal, monetary, or credit policy.

Higher tariffs would reinforce the "deteriorating" banking sector outlooks for China and Hong Kong, according to the ratings firm.

The Chinese government is expected to take initiatives to support the economy, which will maintain pressure on banks' profitability. Hong Kong is expected to see the largest rise in nonperforming loans in Asia-Pacific in 2025, due to a lingering property sector malaise.

The current "neutral" outlooks for the banking sectors in South Korea, Taiwan, and Thailand could move to "deteriorating" if US tariffs substantially increase beyond the 10% tariff imposed on all US trade partners on April 2, or if the impact on their economic growth is more severe than expected.

The profitability and asset quality of the banks in these countries could likely weaken under a higher tariff scenario, given their relatively high exposure to exports and sales to the US, as well as the high tariffs these markets faced under the US President Donald Trump's administration, which were subsequently mostly suspended.

A high-tariff scenario could also lead to lower-than-expected policy rates, which would weigh on their net interest margins, Fitch said.

data.title: Argentina posts $527 mln energy trade surplus in March
data.description:
Argentina posted an energy trade surplus of $527 million in March, the nation's Energy Ministry said on Wednesday, as exports reached $753 million and imports totaled $226 million.

The surplus comes as the country ramps up development of its vast Vaca Muerta shale formation, which holds one of the world's largest unconventional oil and gas reserves.

The increased output has helped ease a long-standing energy deficit and reduced pressure on the central bank's foreign currency reserves.

data.title: Singapore's Non-Oil Domestic Exports Slow More Than Expected
data.description:
By Ronnie Harui

SINGAPORE--Singapore's non-oil domestic exports rose by less than expected in March, as shipments of non-electronics grew at half the pace that was seen in February.

Non-oil domestic exports from the Southeast Asian trading hub rose 5.4% from a year earlier in March, Enterprise Singapore said Thursday. That compared with the median estimate for a 15.2% increase projected in a poll of economists by The Wall Street Journal, and February's 7.6% expansion.

Electronics exports, a key driver of the city-state's economic growth, climbed 11.9% on the year last month, up from February's 6.9% growth. Non-electronics exports increased 3.8% on the year in March, moderating from a revised 7.7% expansion in the prior month.

Products including non-monetary gold, which surged 64.7%, and pharmaceuticals, which jumped 24.9%, contributed the most to the rise in non-electronic NODX, the government agency said.

Enterprise Singapore said it is "actively monitoring the evolving tariff situation and will adjust the 2025 NODX forecast as necessary to reflect changing market conditions."

 
Singapore's Non-Oil Domestic Exports to Top Markets (% Y/Y) 
 
                             March       February 
Taiwan                        45.7          77.9 
Indonesia                     63.0          -5.4 
South Korea                   21.6          31.4 
Malaysia                      12.4          17.3 
EU 27                         11.0          16.7 
US                             5.7          21.5 
Hong Kong                     14.2         -21.2 
Thailand                      12.1          28.1 
Japan                         12.0           4.8 
China                        -29.4         -27.4

Write to Ronnie Harui at ronnie.harui@wsj.com

data.title: Woodside Signs LNG Supply Deal With European Energy Firm
data.description:
Woodside Energy (ASXWDS) signed a liquefied natural gas (LNG) supply agreement with Germany-based energy company Uniper, according to a Thursday filing with the Australian bourse.

The deal includes 1 million tonnes per annum (Mtpa) of LNG from the proposed Louisiana project for up to 13 years, along with 1 Mtpa from Woodside Energy Trading Singapore, starting from the project's commercial operations date and continuing until 2039, the filing said.

The agreements are contingent on a final investment decision for the proposed 16.5 Mtpa, three-train development at the project, the filing added.

Financial terms were not disclosed.

The company's shares were up over 2% in recent Thursday trade.

data.title: Gold continues record rally as trade war woes fuel safe-haven demand
data.description:
Gold extended its record rally on Thursday as investors sought refuge in safe-haven assets amid an intensifying global trade conflict between the United States and its trading partners.

FUNDAMENTALS

* Spot gold was up 0.1% at $3,346.20 an ounce as of 0008 GMT, after touching a record high of $3,357.40 per ounce earlier in the session.

* U.S. gold futures firmed 0.4% to $3,359.50.

* On Wednesday, gold prices pierced the $3,300 an ounce barrier to hit record highs.

* Marking another escalation in his dispute with trade partners, U.S. President Donald Trump on Tuesday ordered a probe into potential new tariffs on all critical minerals imports on top of reviews into pharmaceutical and chip imports.

* Beijing ordered airlines to not take further deliveries of Boeing aircraft, while U.S. government limited exports of Nvidia's H20 artificial intelligence chip to China.

* Gold, traditionally seen as a hedge against political and economic uncertainty and inflation, has risen more than 27% so far this year.

* U.S. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said on Wednesday the Fed would wait for more data on the economy's direction before changing interest rates, but cautioned that Trump's tariff policies risked pushing inflation and employment further from the central bank's goals.

* Traders currently see around 91 basis points worth of rate cuts by 2025-end. (FEDWATCH)

* The dollar, meanwhile, slipped against its rivals to hold near a three-year low hit last week, making gold more attractive for other currency holders.

* Spot silver rose 0.1% to $32.78 an ounce, platinum gained 0.2% to $969.05 and palladium fell 0.7% to $964.75.

DATA/EVENTS (GMT)

1230 US Housing Starts Number Mar

1230 US Initial Jobless Clm 12 Apr, w/e

1230 US Philly Fed Business Indx Apr

1315 EU ECB Refinancing Rate, ECB Deposit Rate Apr

data.title: Valero books $1.1 bln impairment, may idle California refinery
data.description:
Valero Energy Corp said on Wednesday it was taking a $1.1 billion pre-tax impairment related to its California refineries and told state regulators it would permanently shut or restructure its San Francisco-area refinery in Benicia, California by the end of April 2026.

The move comes as refiners face growing regulatory and cost pressures in California, the largest U.S. gasoline market, where the state's emissions targets and proposed refinery transparency rules have weighed on long-term investment decisions.

The Benicia converts an average of 145,000 barrels per day (bpd) of crude oil into motor fuels, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration.

"We understand the impact that this may have on our employees, business partners, and community, and will continue to work with them through this period," Lane Riggs, chief executive of San Antonio-based Valero, said on Wednesday.

Valero also said on Wednesday it was weighing strategic options for its 91,300 bpd Los Angeles-area refinery.

Riggs said in October that the company was keeping all options on the table for its two California refineries.

The number of refineries in California processing crude oil has been shrinking with companies citing increased regulation like the state's plans to ban the sale of gasoline-powered automobiles by 2035. Six plants have shut since 2008. Two of those have converted to producing renewable diesel.

Competing U.S. refiner Phillips 66 , also in October said it would shutter its 139,000 bpd Los Angeles refinery within a year. That announcement came days after a state law was signed by Governor Gavin Newsom requiring refineries to keep stockpiles of fuel on hand to limit price spikes.

Gasoline prices in California are among the highest in the United States due to the state's reliance on West Coast refineries or imports from Asia or the Middle East. Unlike other U.S. regions, there are no pipelines connecting California with refining centers along the Gulf Coast or in the Midwest.

The Benicia refinery in the northeast of San Francisco Bay has a throughput capacity of 145,000 barrels per day.

data.title: Singapore March exports rise 5.4% y/y, less than forecast
data.description:
Singapore's non-oil domestic exports rose 5.4% in March from the same month a year earlier, government data showed on Thursday, with shipments of both electronics and non-electronics increasing.

The rise compared with a Reuters poll forecast of growth of 14.1% for March, and followed a 7.6% rise in February.

Details of the month-on-month seasonally adjusted change in exports were not included in Enterprise Singapore's statement.

Exports to Taiwan, Indonesia, Hong Kong, Thailand, Japan and South Korea increased in annual terms in March, while exports to China declined.

The trade minister said on Wednesday a recession could not be ruled out in the trade-driven city-state because of U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff policy.

Also on Wednesday, Prime Minister Lawrence Wong said there was little comfort in the U.S. postponing most of its proposed "reciprocal" tariffs for three months, saying the changes had already created great uncertainty around the world.

On Monday, the central bank loosened monetary policy for the second time this year and the trade ministry lowered its economic growth forecast for 2025.

data.title: Japanese Shares Rise on Tech Rebound
data.description:
The Nikkei 225 Index rose 0.4% to above 34,000 while the broader Topix Index gained 0.3% to 2,505 on Thursday, recouping losses from the previous session, with technology stocks leading the rebound.

This upward move came despite a sharp selloff on Wall Street overnight, where US Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell warned that tariffs could challenge the central bank’s dual mandate of controlling inflation and supporting growth.

At home, data showed Japan’s export growth in March fell short of expectations, weighed down by weaker shipments to China and the EU. However, import growth returned to positive territory, suggesting some resilience in domestic demand.

Leading tech gains were Disco Corp (+1%), Advantest (+1.8%), and Lasertec (+2.7%).

Other major names also rose, including Mitsubishi Heavy (+1.5%), Hitachi (+1.2%), and Fast Retailing (+1%).

data.title: Insignia extends exclusivity window for Bain and CC Capital bids
data.description:
Australia's Insignia Financial said on Thursday that it has extended the exclusivity period by four weeks for both its bidders, Bain Capital and CC Capital Partners, to consider their proposals to acquire the wealth manager.

Following the requests of both Bain and CC Capital, Insignia extended the exclusivity period to allow the two suitors to finalise debt funding and associated due diligence, the company said in a statement.

Shares of Insignia rose 3.9% to A$3.73 by 0030 GMT, while the broader benchmark ASX 200 index inched 0.1% higher.

Earlier in March, the company granted access to its books to Bain, best known in Australia for acquiring Virgin Australia (IPO-VIR.AX) out of administration, and CC Capital, a Wall Street-based private equity firm, after both raised their respective takeover bids to A$3.34 billion ($2.13 billion).

($1 = 1.5711 Australian dollars)

data.title: West African Resources Reports Lower Q1 Gold Production; Shares Hit All-Time High
data.description:
West African Resources reported first-quarter gold production of 50,033 ounces at an all-in-sustaining cost of $1,262 per ounce, compared with the 51,178 ounces produced in the fourth quarter of 2024, according to a Thursday Australian bourse filing.

The company's first quarter unhedged gold sales came in at 48,338 ounces at an average price of $2,832 per ounce, compared with the 47,953 ounces sold in the previous quarter.

It completed a maiden grade control drilling program at the Kiaka gold project in Burkina Faso, with mining scheduled to ramp up in the second quarter. It progressed construction work on the project.

The company's unhedged annual gold production guidance for 2025 is 290,000 to 360,000 ounces.

West African Resources' shares rose past 2% in recent Thursday trade, earlier hitting an all-time high.

data.title: JGBs Consolidate, With Focus on U.S.-Japan Trade TalksMarket Talk
data.description: JGBs consolidate in the morning Tokyo session, with focus on U.S.-Japan trade talks. Japanese government bond prices are likely to continue rising today, Mitsubishi UFJ Morgan Stanley Securities fixed income strategists say in a research report. However, as the outcome of U.S.-Japan negotiations over tariffs becomes clear, the market could move in either a risk-on or risk-off direction, the strategists say. Markets will likely keep a close eye on the situation, they add. The 5-year JGB yield is up 0.5bp at 0.840%; the 10-year yield is unchanged at 1.290%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Fed's Schmid says patience needed to see how tariffs play out
data.description:
The Federal Reserve faces a complicated picture for policy decisions, Kansas City Federal Reserve Bank President Jeff Schmid said on Wednesday, noting that while he is hearing nervousness about tariffs in the agricultural sector, a recent decline in energy prices may deliver an offsetting "shot in the arm" that could somewhat limit inflation.

"I just think that we've got to, we've got to be a little patient to see how some of these things play out," Schmid said in a webcast conversation with Dallas Federal Reserve Bank President Lorie Logan at a Dallas Fed event.

At the end of the day, he said, the Fed will "react in a positive way to any of those disruptions that might affect that dual mandate," referring to the Fed's mandates of achieving both price stability and full employment.

Earlier on Wednesday, Fed Chair Jerome Powell said the U.S. central bank would wait for more data on the economy before considering any change in rates. But he warned that President Donald Trump's tariff policies risk both higher inflation and higher unemployment that could potentially force the Fed into choosing which fight to prioritize.

Schmid and Logan did not have an in-depth discussion on interest-rate policy or of recent market volatility as investors digest the implications of Trump's trade policy.

Schmid briefly touched on the importance of the Fed's political independence, which he said is what allows Fed policymakers to set rates with nothing but the financial and economic stability of Americans in mind.

"Everything is being challenged now. ... All the firewalls we thought we had have turned into screen doors," he said. But, he added, "I think you are tested by fire ... and frankly the people I talk to, both politicians and the public, seem to be pleased with that structure, so I'm very optimistic."

data.title: PBOC is expected to set the USD/CNY reference rate at 7.3083 – Reuters estimate
data.description:
People's Bank of China USD/CNY reference rate is due around 0115 GMT.

The People's Bank of China (PBOC), China's central bank, is responsible for setting the daily midpoint of the yuan (also known as renminbi or RMB). The PBOC follows a managed floating exchange rate system that allows the value of the yuan to fluctuate within a certain range, called a "band," around a central reference rate, or "midpoint." It's currently at +/- 2%.

How the process works:

  • Daily midpoint setting: Each morning, the PBOC sets a midpoint for the yuan against a basket of currencies, primarily the US dollar. The central bank takes into account factors such as market supply and demand, economic indicators, and international currency market fluctuations. The midpoint serves as a reference point for that day's trading.
  • The trading band: The PBOC allows the yuan to move within a specified range around the midpoint. The trading band is set at +/- 2%, meaning the yuan could appreciate or depreciate by a maximum of 2% from the midpoint during a single trading day. This range is subject to change by the PBOC based on economic conditions and policy objectives.
  • Intervention: If the yuan's value approaches the limit of the trading band or experiences excessive volatility, the PBOC may intervene in the foreign exchange market by buying or selling the yuan to stabilize its value. This helps maintain a controlled and gradual adjustment of the currency's value.

data.title: Vietnam says to boost power capacity in $136 bln plan
data.description:
Vietnam aims to raise its total installed power generation capacity to 183 to 236 gigawatts by 2030, up from more than 80 GW at the end of 2023, the government said late on Wednesday, citing details of an amended national power development plan.

The plan will focus on renewable energy development and will for the first time add nuclear power to the mix, the government said in a statement.

data.title: Japan's Exports Rose in March, But Pace Slowed Sharply
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa

TOKYO--Japan's exports rose for a sixth straight month in March but at a much slower pace as concerns over U.S. President Trump's tariffs start to weigh on global trade.

Exports climbed 3.9% in March from a year earlier, compared with February's 11.4% increase, data released by the Ministry of Finance showed Thursday. Economists polled by data provider FactSet had forecast a 4.3% rise.

With tariffs on key products like cars and electronics, Japan's export growth may slow further in the near term after a temporary boost from pre-tariff rush demand during the first quarter.

Exports to the U.S. slowed sharply, rising just 3.1% in March from a year earlier on demand for cars and electronic parts, after a 10.5% increase in the previous month.

As Tokyo negotiates with the Trump administration to seek an exemption from additional tariffs, economists said they expect Japanese automakers to hold back on shipping cars to the U.S., which are subject to a 25% tariff. Motor vehicles account for about 30% of Japan's shipments to the U.S.

Trump has said he is considering a short-term exemption for automakers to give them more time to move production to the U.S.

Economists expect the Japanese government to prepare a package to fill the trade gap between the two countries, which may include increasing purchases of American agricultural products, defense equipment and energy.

Japan reported a 847 billion yen trade surplus with the U.S. in March, equivalent to $5.97 billion.

Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com

data.title: Aside From Cannington, South32 3Q Output in LineMarket Talk
data.description: South32's 3Q production was in line with market expectations with the exception of its Cannington mine, Jefferies analyst Mitch Ryan says in a note. "While we expect pricing for sales to remain somewhat volatile with the overhang of tariffs and shifting global supply chains, FX tailwinds and stable operating conditions suggest H2 margins should remain relatively robust," Ryan says. Jefferies has a hold rating and A$3.20 target on the stock, which is down 0.8% at A$2.65. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

Roto Pumps Launches New Product
data.description:
Roto Pumps launched a new product on Wednesday named the P-Range pump, which is a progressive cavity pump, according to a same-day filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The new product has been launched in the Indian and international markets.
